notes: SHARED-NAT / EGRESS-SATURATION TRAP. All outbound (internet-bound) traffic from the az1 subnet leaves through a single shared NAT gateway net:nat-gw-az1, which has a finite pool of SNAT source ports per destination and a fixed egress bandwidth. After a deploy, the data-export batch job began a large continuous outbound sync to an object store, holding thousands of long-lived outbound connections and saturating both the SNAT port pool (ErrorPortAllocation) and egress bandwidth on nat-gw-az1. Three UNRELATED services that each make OUTBOUND third-party calls through the same NAT -- payment-gateway-client (calls Stripe api.stripe.com), maps-svc (calls a geocoder), push-notifier (calls APNs) -- all start timing out on new outbound connections at the same moment because no SNAT ports are free. They share NO service-call edge in service_map.json: they sit in different call graphs and only co-depend on the NAT egress path (each lists egress via net:nat-gw-az1 and a distinct external dependency). payment-gateway-client is LOUDEST/paged (checkout failures). Control services catalog-svc (internal-only, own Postgres, no external egress) and internal-api (no egress) do NOT use the NAT and stay healthy, proving the fault is the shared NAT egress, not a global outage. Origin is the NAT gateway egress saturation; data-export is the heavy tenant whose outbound flood triggered it.
_design_note: Paged on payment-gateway-client because checkout failures are the most revenue-visible symptom. payment-gateway-client is one of three services whose OUTBOUND calls timed out simultaneously; check WHY (the shared NAT gateway SNAT port allocation errors / egress bandwidth at cap), not just payment-gateway-client's own logs. image-resizer errored and just deployed but is not on the NAT egress path -- its fault is local.
